Author: Pete Graham
Title: My thoughts on AI coding tools like Github Copilot, Cursor, and Lovable | Pete Graham posted on the topic | LinkedIn
Text:
There's a lot of hype about Vibe Coding, I've been using AI coding tools since Github Copilot came out and more recently Cursor and Lovable, here are my brief thoughts on them as someone who's been writing software since he had hair: 1. ✍️🧠 Writing is thinking. Coding is writing 2. 💡⏱️ The innovation here is a shorter feedback loop between your thoughts and working software. Agile, CD and cloud computing all also provided shorter feedback loops and the opportunity for greater experimentation 3. 💼👩💻 When you manage developers, you probably don't check every line of code they write, but you do need to be effective at describing what you want (and to a lesser extent, how it should be done). It is possible for people with a non-technical background to work very well with developers, for example, some product managers. These people will likely also excel at coding with AI tools 4. 🐛🧘♀️ Bugs are normal, every new developer learns this, as the software they create gets more complex bugs compound. Thankfully, we've developed techniques to deal with this, primarily automated testing. In my opinion, we're in the phase of working out how we can map these techniques to AI coding workflows. This is what we need for these tools to be truly useful for production code
I love the analogy of writing as thinking. The shorter feedback loop between thought and code is definitely a game changer. As these tools evolve, how do you see AI’s role in helping developers with debugging and refining the code?
Pete Graham have you tried pair programming and perhaps TDD approach with what ever LLM agent Myself I have been using it to build CLI experiments to vet something before building out UI, I do think the boom for developers is CLIs and an explosion of voice UX
